coredata.dc:title: One-step synthesis of orange luminescent carbon dots for Ag+ sensing and cell imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Zhong, Yin
Li, Junjian
Jiao, Yingzhi
Zuo, Gancheng
Pan, Xihao
Su, Ting
Dong, Wei
coredata.dc:description: A fast and facile approach to synthesize orange luminescent carbon dots by hydrothermal method, while 1,2-benzenediamine and carbamaldehyde serve as precursors. In order to get stronger fluorescence emission, doping N heteroatoms to make N-CDs. The N-CDs exhibit a high quantum yield of 14.3% along with the fluorescence lifetime of 2.86ns. Because the emission of N-CDs is quenched by Ag+ efficiently, N-CDs can be employed as a highly sensitive and selective detection for Ag+ detection and N-CDs were exploited for the determination of Ag+ in environmental water samples with satisfactory results. Significantly, using MTT assay, N-CDs have low toxicity against 293T cells and N-CDs have been applied for A549 cell imaging successfully.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Hydrothermal method
Ag+ detection
Cell imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of N-CDs preparation based on 1,2-benzenediamine and carbamaldehyde.
(a)TEM image and particle size distribution histogram of N-CDs; (b) XRD pattern with Cu Kα radiation (λ=0.154nm) in the 2θ range of 0–80°.
XPS survey-scan spectrum (a), high-resolution C1s (b), and N1s (c) XPS spectra of N-CDs (d) FTIR spectra of N-CDs.

(a) Normalized UV−vis absorption (blue line), FL emission (red line, λ em = 568nm), and FL excitation (black line, λ ex = 380nm) spectra; (b) Changes of the emission peak when the excitation wavelengths changes from 330nm to 490nm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Fluorescence decay of N-CDs in aqueous solution.

(a) Fluorescence intensity of different metal ions including 1 Ag + 2K+, 3 Ni2+, 4 Ca2+, 5 Fe2+, 6 Zn2+, 7 Cu2+, 8 Mg2+, 9 Hg2+, 10 Ba2+, 11 Na+, 12Cd2+, 13 Cr3+ and 14 Fe3+ were added into N-CDs and N-CDs/Ag+ solution; (b) Flourescence intensity of N-CDs with Ag + 0–40μM).
Cytotoxicity test of 293T cells using the standard MTT assay with N-CDs.

Cellular images of A549: (a) fluorescence image of A549 cells after been incubated with 30μL 50μg/mL N-CDs for 24h and its bright field (b); (c) fluorescence image of A549 cells incubated with 30μL 50μg/mL N-CDs for 24h and further added Ag+ (0.10mM) for 12h and its bright field (d).
Determination results of N-CDs in river water (n = 4).

head.ce:title: One-step synthesis of orange luminescent carbon dots for Ag+ sensing and cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanjing University of Science and Technology, School of Chemical Engineering, Nanjing 210094, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanjing University of Science and Technology, School of Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Scientific Research, Nanjing Huawe Medicine Technology Development co., LTD, Nanjing 210023, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Scientific Research, Nanjing Huawe Medicine Technology Development co., LTD

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A fast and facile approach to synthesize orange luminescent carbon dots by hydrothermal method, while 1,2-benzenediamine and carbamaldehyde serve as precursors. In order to get stronger fluorescence emission, doping N heteroatoms to make N-CDs. The N-CDs exhibit a high quantum yield of 14.3% along with the fluorescence lifetime of 2.86ns. Because the emission of N-CDs is quenched by Ag+ efficiently, N-CDs can be employed as a highly sensitive and selective detection for Ag+ detection and N-CDs were exploited for the determination of Ag+ in environmental water samples with satisfactory results. Significantly, using MTT assay, N-CDs have low toxicity against 293T cells and N-CDs have been applied for A549 cell imaging successfully.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have attracted a lot of attention for varied promising applications in fields of sensor, nanometer domain and drug delivery, because of its special structure [1,2]. Carbon dots (CDs) usually exist in the size of less 10nm with excellent properties such as satisfactory fluorescent performance, low cytotoxicity and biocompatibility owing to their specific nanometer dimension [3–6]. Due to these advantages, Carbon dots (CDs) can be used in metal ion detection such as Ag+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Cu2+ and biological application [7–17]. As such, it is meaningful to explore the better synthesis methods and applications of Carbon dots (CDs).

Until now, the synthesis methods to compound CDs can be generally divided into two categories,top–down approaches [9,18] and bottom–up approaches [19,20]. The top–down approaches contain electrochemical oxidation, acidicoxidation, arc discharge and laser ablation [21–23]. On the other hand, the bottom-up methods include hydrothermal, microwave [24], and ultrasonic, which have been normally applied to synthesize CDs [25,26]. However, the most of CDs show a relatively low fluorescence quantum-yield (QY) compared with conventional semiconductor QDs [24,27,28]. Many attempts are made to dope CDs with heteroatoms such as nitrogen (N), boron (B), sulfur (S), phosphorous (P) and fluorine (F) in order to achieve CDs with different properties and extend their application [29,30]. Recently, it is reported that doped CDs such as N-doped CDs and P-doped CDs can increase the fluorescence of CDs [31–35]. Thus, it is needed to continue to search for a facile, effective and green synthesis methods of doped CDs.

In this study, we employed 1,2-benzenediamine and carbamaldehyde serving as precursors to synthesize orange fluorescent N-doped CDs [36] (N-CDs) by hydrothermal method ( Fig. 1). The prepared orange N-CDs have great water-solubility and show low toxicity in cells at low concentrations. The N-CDs exhibit a high quantum yield of 14.3% along with the fluorescence lifetime of 2.86ns. In PBS (pH = 7.4) buffer, N-CDs have good sensitivity and selectivity to Ag+ in the range of 0–40µm and the limit of detection (LOD) of N-CDs for Ag+ is 2 × 10−8 mol/L(Fig. S1). Besides, N-CDs have been proved as an effective fluorescent sensor even in the environmental water samples. Because of well stability of the N-CDs, N-CDs solution remains photostability at room temperature for one month (Fig. S2). Finally, because of its low toxicity, N-CDs can be used in A549 cell imaging successfully.

1,2-benzenediamine,carbamaldehyde and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were supplied by Alfa-Aesar (Beijing, China). Fetal bovine serum (FBS), dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM) and 3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)−2,5-diphenyl-2-H-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) were supplied from Key-GEN BioTECH (Jiangsu, China). The solutions of all experiments were used deionized water. All materials were used without any further purification.

0.5g 1,2-benzenediamine and 0.5g carbamaldehyde were dissolved in 30mL deionized water. The mixture was reacted at 180°C for 3h in a 50mL autoclave. After the reaction completed, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature and the mixture purified through column chromatography. The purified solution used rotary evaporator to dry at 60°C, removing water and other small molecules. Finally, the prepared N-CDs was stored at 4°C for further use.

The transmission electron microscopic (TEM) analyses were tested on a Tecnai G2 T2 S-TWIN transmission electron microscopy (TEM) with an accelerating voltage of 200kV. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was performed on a XRD instrument (DMAX-2200) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.154nm) in the 2θ range of 0–80°and at a voltage of 30kV and 20mA. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of the N-CDs were recorded on a Nicolet iS10 FTIR instrument (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), working in attenuated total reflectance mode, within a spectral range of 400–4000cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was using a thermoelectron instrument (Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250) with Al Kαradiation (hν=1486.6eV). The UV–Vis spectra were recorded with a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrometer at 200–700nm. Fluorescent spectra were performed on a Shimadzu RF-5301 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The quantum yield (QY) of N-CDs was measured according to the established procedure. Briefly, quinine sulfate in 0.1M H2SO4 (literature quantum yield is 0.54 at 380nm) was chosen as a standard. The QY of N-CDs was calculated according to the equation:
Y
u
=
Y
s
×
(
F
u
/
F
s
)
×
(
A
s
/
A
u
)
×
(
η
u
2
/
η
s
2
)
Where “s” means the standard and “u” means the sample. Y is the QY, F is the measured integrated emission intensity, A is the absorbance and η is the refractive index of the solvent.
η
u
=
η
s
= 1.33. Fluorescence lifetime was measured with a HORIBA-FL3-111.

Different metal ions including K+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Hg2+, Ba2+, Na+, Cd2+, Cr3+ and Fe3+ were added into N-CDs and N-CDs/Ag+ solution to explore the influence of different metal ions to Ag+ quenching effect. 100μL 10mM metal ions were added into 900μL buffer including 100μL of N-CDs or 100μL of C-NDs/Ag+ solution and 800μL of PBS buffer (pH=7.4). After reaction for 5min at room temperature, the fluorescence measurements were carried out using an excitation wavelength at 380nm. It also explored the influence of Inorganic salt and pH to quenching effect. In order to explore the detection of Ag+, different concentrations of Ag+ (0−40μM) were added into N-CDs solution.

For the cell cytotoxicity test, N-CDs were tested by MTT in human lung cancer (A549) cells. A549 cells were seeded on 96-well culture plates at an initial density of 5×103 cells/well and incubated at 37°C under 5% CO2 overnight. The old DMEM sucked out and 100μL DMEM of 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) containing different concentrations of N-CDs (0, 10, 20, 30, 50,100μL/mL) were added into the wells incubating for another 24h. After adding 20μL of 5.0mg/mL MTT reagent into every well, the cells were further incubated for 4h. The medium was then removed and 200μL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was added to make the formazan salt soluble. The optical density (OD) of each well was measured at 490nm with BioTek® Synergy H4.

Human lung cancer (A549) cells were seeded into a 12-well culture plates a an initial density of 5×103 cells/well and incubated at 37°C under 5% CO2 overnight. Then, the A549 cells were incubated with 30μL 50μg/mL N-CDs solution at 37°C under 5% CO2 for 24h. After incubation, using PBS (pH=7.4) to wash the cells three times. The cells were photographed at different excitation wavelength using TCS SP5 laser scanning confocal microscopy (CLSM) (Leica, Germany). In addition, Ag+ (0.10mM) was added in each well incubating 12h and TCS SP5 laser scanning confocal microscopy (CLSM) (Leica, Germany) was used to observe cells.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to explore the morphology of the N-CDs. The TEM image ( Fig. 2a) shows that the N-CDs have spherical shapes. Besides, a narrow size distribution with an average size of around 4.75nm can be seen in the Fig. 2a. The XRD pattern of the N-CDs in Fig. 2b showed a broad peak (002) centered at around 23°, corresponding to an interlayer spacing d of 0.386nm, which was consistent with the (002) lattice spacing of carbon-based materials with turbostratic disorder. The XRD results imply that the prepared N-CDs are amorphous, which consist of disordered carbon cores with an amorphous surface structure. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to explore the chemical constitution and surface state of N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 3a b c, the XPS survey scan spectrum of N-CDs has three typical peaks of C1s, N1s and O1s. The C 1s high-resolution XPS spectrum reveals three types of carbon bonds of C˭C/C−C, C−N and C˭O at 284.90, 285.93 and 286.58eV. As the same, the N 1s high-resolution XPS spectrum exhibits the different peaks of pyridine-like N, amino N, and pyrrolic N at 399.09, 400.05, and 401.10eV. Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) was recorded to identify the surface chemical groups and structure of N-CDs (Fig. 3d). The FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs shows the major amine absorption bands (3123, 3056cm−1), methylene (2789cm−1), amide carbonyl (1689cm−1), C˭C (1455cm−1), C−N (1405cm−1), and aromatic C−NH (1240cm−1) functional groups or chemical bonds. From the results of XPS and FTIR spectra, it can be substantiated clearly that N atoms had perfectly doped in N-CDs.

In order to explore the spectral properties of N-CDs, the UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence properties were needed. The UV spectrum of N-CDs ( Fig. 4a blue line) has two peaks at 270nm and 400nm which means π−π* and n−π transitions, respectively. Fig. 4a red line shows N-CDs having an excitation-independent feature with the emission maximum at ca. 560nm. The FL excitation spectrum of N-CDs (Fig. 4a black line) displays a wavelength maximum at ca.380nm, which indicates that the observed orange emission maybe be triggered by the carboxyl and/or C−N structures in N-CDs. Fig. 4b indicates when the excitation wavelengths changes from 330nm to 490nm, the emission peak does not shift but the fluorescence intensity decreases and the maximum fluorescence intensity is at ac.560nm with an excitation wavelength of 380nm. N-CDs can be used in bio-imaging, because of this excitation independent fluorescence behavior. Using quinine sulfate as a reference chromophore, the QY of N-CDs of 14.3% is obtained under the excitation at 380nm which the high quantum yield may be ascribed to the nitrogen doping. Moreover, the average fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs was calculated as 2.86ns ( Fig. 5).

When pH changes from 2 to 10, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs has no obvious change, but when pH changes to more than 10 the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs decreases quickly which means N-CDs have strong orange luminescent only at pH<10 (Fig. S3). The effect of the pH values can be understood in terms of the change in surface charge owing to deprotonation and indicates that the N-CDs can be used in both acidic and weak alkaline environments. As showed in Fig. S3, the concentrations of inorganic salt have no influence to N-CDs, indicating the good stability of N-CDs even in high ionic environment. Different metal ions including K+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Hg2+, Ba2+, Na+, Cd2+, Cr3+ and Fe3+ were used to explore the influence of different metal ions to quenching effect of Ag+. We find that the Ag+ has quenching effect to N-CDs at these metal ions ( Fig. 6a). As showed in Fig. 6b, when the Ag+ in the concentrations range of 0–40µm was added into N-CDs solution, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs decreases obviously at about 550nm. Therefore, N-CDs can be used to develop a stable fluorescence Sensor of Ag+.

In order to test the application of N-CDs for Ag+ sensing in river water, the N-CDs were applied in the measure of Ag+ in river water samples which were spiked with standard Ag+ solutions. Table 1 shows that the recovery study of Ag+ is measured by the N-CDs. The results show that the N-CDs have quantitative measure of Ag+ in river water.

The low toxicity of N-CDs is the most important point for biotechnological application excepting photoluminescence intensity and good water solubility. Hence, we use the 293T cell line to evaluate the toxicity of N-CDs through MTT assay. 293T cell line were incubated with different concentrations of N-CDs between the scope of 0–100μgmL −1 for one day ( Fig. 7). The 293T cells showed the cell viability to be over 85%. The results of MTT assay proved that these N-CDs did have low toxicity and even at a real high concentration (100μgmL −1) of N-CDs the cells survived more than 85%. Despite the toxicity of N-CDs increases when the concentrations increase, these N-CDs have shown a lower toxicity than those reported C-Dots prepared from PEG [37]. Hence, N-CDs can be used in some biotechnological applications.

Because of the low toxicity and obvious fluorescence performance of N-CDs, the N-CDs maybe can be uses in bio-imaging. Herein, A549 cells were incubated with N-CDs and N-CDs/Ag+ for 24h. Afterwards, the A549 cellular imaging was photographed by confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM). The A549 cells incubating with N-CDs are obvious to see the intense green fluorescence at 465nm lasers ( Fig. 8a), meanwhile cells incubated with N-CDs/Ag+ show no green fluorescence at 465nm lasers (Fig. 8c). The white light images of the A549 cells (Fig. 8b.d) showed that the cells have normal appearance with or without Ag+, which can explain the biocompatibility and low toxicity of N-CDs.

In summary, we used 1,2-Benzenediamine and Carbamaldehyde to synthesize orange luminescent carbon dots by hydrothermal method. This method is fast and easy and the synthesized N-CDs have size of 4.75nm on average. The N-CDs exhibit a high quantum yield of 14.3% along with the fluorescence lifetime of 2.86ns. Additionally N-CDs show excitation independent emission and can be used as a highly sensitive sensor for Ag+ detection and further measure environmental river water samples with satisfactory results. In PBS (pH = 7.4) buffer, N-CDs have good sensitivity and selectivity to Ag+ in the range of 0–40µm and the limit of detection (LOD) of N-CDs for Ag+ is 2 × 10−8 mol/L. More importantly, because N-CDs have low toxicity to cells, N-CDs can be applied for cells imaging and other biological applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) (Grant number 51573078).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2017.05.057.
